core_meaning: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える means something is visible or comes into view — it describes what naturally enters your sight, not an active choice to look. It can also mean 'to seem / to appear' when describing how something looks to you subjectively.

sentences.jp: ここから<ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えるよ。
sentences.translation: You can see Mt. Fuji from here.
sentences.notes: Core 'visible from a location' use — the mountain is passively in view, not actively looked at. <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える here expresses a natural state.

sentences.jp: <ruby>霧<rt>きり</rt></ruby>が<ruby>濃<rt>こ</rt></ruby>くて、<ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>全然<rt>ぜんぜん</rt></ruby><ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えなかった。
sentences.translation: The fog was so thick I couldn't see anything ahead.
sentences.notes: Shows the negative — something is NOT visible due to conditions, a natural and common use of <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える.

sentences.jp: あの<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>、<ruby>実際<rt>じっさい</rt></ruby>より<ruby>若<rt>わか</rt></ruby>く<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えるよね。
sentences.translation: That person looks younger than they actually are, don't they?
sentences.notes: 'To appear/seem' sense — describing a subjective impression of how something or someone looks to the speaker.

sentences.jp: <ruby>遠<rt>とお</rt></ruby>くに<ruby>小<rt>ちい</rt></ruby>さな<ruby>島<rt>しま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えてきた。
sentences.translation: A small island came into view in the distance.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えてきた captures the moment something gradually enters view — a natural and vivid collocation.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>資料<rt>しりょう</rt></ruby>からは、<ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>の<ruby>本質<rt>ほんしつ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えてくる。
sentences.translation: From this material, the true nature of the problem becomes apparent.
sentences.notes: Abstract/figurative use — something conceptual 'becomes visible' or apparent, common in written and analytical contexts.

dont_use: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える is for passive visibility or seeming — don't use it when you mean actively looking at something; that's <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る (e.g. テレビを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る, NOT テレビが<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える). Also avoid confusing it with <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる, which means 'to show' (an active, transitive action). A common mistake is using <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える with を — this word takes が for the thing being seen.

confused_with.contrast: Learners often say 「テレビを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える」 when they mean to watch TV — but <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える cannot take を and describes involuntary visibility, not intentional watching. Use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る (with を) when you actively choose to look at or watch something: 「テレビを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る」is natural, 「テレビが<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える」means 'the TV is visible / I can see the TV (from here).'

confusion_set.use_when: Something is passively visible, or something/someone seems/appears a certain way
confusion_set.example: <ruby>窓<rt>まど</rt></ruby>から<ruby>海<rt>うみ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える。

confusion_set.use_when: You actively look at or watch something (takes を)
You show something to someone (transitive, intentional display)
confusion_set.example: <ruby>写真<rt>しゃしん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>友達<rt>ともだち</rt></ruby>に<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せた。

frequency: Very common in everyday speech and writing. The 'visible' sense appears constantly in conversation; the 'seem/appear' sense is equally frequent and natural across all registers.
anki_hint: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える takes が (not を) — if something is passively in view or 'seems' a certain way, reach for <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える; if you're actively watching, use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る.
